one_line_takeaway: Security-aware, CWE-specific LLM prompting with contrastive chain-of-thought outperforms both vanilla prompts and static analyzers (CodeGuru, Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQube) at detecting vulnerabilities in partial code, though absolute accuracy on real-world data remains modest.
key_finding: For every one of the 4 CWEs there exists an LLM prompting strategy that beats BOTH the vanilla-prompt baseline and the static-analyzer baseline, and no single strategy dominates universally (though NL+CoT S3 is the most reliably strong across model/CWE combinations, and NL S1 gives the best averaged pairwise accuracy). With SOTA reasoning models (notably DeepSeek-R1), every prompting strategy exceeds the static-analyzer baseline. The best strategies improve, relative to the static analyzer, accuracy by up to 31.6% (CWE-78, o1 + NL S1), F1 by up to 71.7% (CWE-190, DeepSeek-R1 + NL+CoT S0), pairwise accuracy by up to 60.4% (CWE-78, o1 + NL S1), and reduce FNR by up to 37.6% (CWE-190). Static analyzers were shown to be nearly useless on partial code: pairwise accuracy was ~0 for essentially all CWEs (highest observed ~3%), e.g. CodeGuru on CWE-78 had 40% precision but only 6.3% recall (F1 ~11%, pAcc 0), and on CWE-476 achieved 100% precision but detected only 1.4% of cases; CWE-190/416 accuracies of ~50% simply reflect the tools defaulting to 'non-vulnerable'.
core_contribution: The paper proposes a family of security-aware LLM prompting strategies for vulnerability detection in the PARTIAL-CODE setting (isolated snippets/functions, not full buildable projects) and empirically shows they can serve as an alternative/complement to conventional static analyzers. The authors' own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy of prompt strategies built up from a Basic (B) prompt, through four Natural-Language-instruction settings (NL S0-S3, differing in where the CWE instruction comes from: CWE-ID only, LLM-generated, LLM-generated from few-shot SVEN examples, and verbatim MITRE text), to four NL+Contrastive-Chain-of-Thought settings (NL+CoT S0-S3) that embed contrastive vulnerable/fixed code pairs plus LLM-generated reasoning; (2) a concrete 4-phase pipeline (Phase 1 pairwise sample selection from Juliet C/C++, Phase 2 GPT-4 instruction generation, Phase 3 GPT-4 contrastive CoT reasoning, Phase 4 manual synthesis of a contrastive CoT template) for generating CWE-specific prompts from open-source CWE information; and (3) an evaluation across 4 CWEs and multiple LLMs showing that for each CWE some prompting strategy beats both a vanilla-prompt baseline and static analyzers, with an emphasis on 'pairwise accuracy' as the fairest metric. NOTE (avoiding related-work confusion): the graph/transformer detectors (Devign, ReVeal, IVDetect, CodeBERT, LineVul), the MoE/CWE-clustering ideas (Yang et al., Ji et al.), and the hybrid SA+LLM line are all CITED prior work in Section VI, NOT this paper's method. This paper's own method is purely inference-time prompting with no fine-tuning.
threat_model: The security concern is undetected software vulnerabilities in partial/incomplete code, an increasingly common setting because ~30% of code at large firms is now AI-generated and up to ~40% of code from some coding assistants reportedly contains security flaws (figures the authors cite from industry reports, not their own measurement). Concretely: developers review code in fragments during pull requests, reuse open-source snippets, and LLM coding assistants generate code line-by-line, so a defender cannot run whole-project static/dynamic analysis. Malicious actors also submit only partial code to LLM assistants and expect partial code back. The 4 target weaknesses are all in MITRE's CWE Top 25: CWE-78 OS Command Injection, CWE-190 Integer Overflow, CWE-476 NULL Pointer Dereference, CWE-416 Use After Free. The paper's framing is defensive detection (flag vulnerable snippets), not exploitation; it does not construct or evaluate an adversary who tries to evade the detector.
limitations: Cost was a major constraint (190,000+ API calls), restricting the study to just 4 CWEs and preventing a comprehensive cross-CWE analysis. The choice of CWEs was further constrained by requiring the CWE to appear in BOTH high-quality real-world (SVEN) and synthetic (Juliet) datasets. On the real-world CVEFixes C/C++ benchmark the absolute gains over Khare et al. are modest (e.g. CWE-190 0.48 to 0.60, CWE-416 0.46 to 0.56, but CWE-78 only 0.63 to 0.67 and CWE-476 only 0.58 to 0.59), and absolute accuracies stay in the 0.56-0.67 range, so LLM prompting is far from solving real-world detection. Some GPT models referenced by prior work were deprecated, so replacement models were substituted, weakening the head-to-head replication. My own reviewer caveats: the method still depends on expert-validated CWE descriptions and manually curated Juliet pairs (not fully automatic), and no adaptive/evasion adversary is evaluated. Pairwise accuracies remain low in absolute terms (e.g. best CWE-416 pAcc only 26.2%), so 'beats static analysis' partly reflects how poorly SA does on partial code rather than strong standalone detection.

datasets_used: SVEN (real-world, manually labeled, ~94% label accuracy; 520 samples filtered to the 4 CWEs: CWE-78 76, CWE-190 114, CWE-416 140, CWE-476 190, each balanced vulnerable/non-vulnerable)
CVEFixes (real-world, ~39.8k total; 1,784 samples used across the 4 CWEs; C/C++ subset of 1,201 samples used for the Khare et al. comparison)
Juliet C/C++ Dynamic Test Suite (synthetic; used only to extract contrastive vulnerable/fixed pairs for prompt construction, one scenario per CWE)
quantitative_results: Best gains vs static analyzer: accuracy +31.6% (CWE-78), F1 +71.7% (CWE-190), pairwise accuracy +60.4% (CWE-78), FNR reduced by up to 37.6% (CWE-190)

Top settings (Table IV, SVEN, avg of 3 trials): CWE-78 o1+NL(S1) pAcc 60.4, Acc 80.0, F1 21.3; CWE-190 DeepSeek-R1+NL+CoT(S0) pAcc 37.7, Acc 65.8, F1 71.7; CWE-476 DeepSeek-R1+NL(S0) pAcc 32.4, Acc 62.8, F1 49.3; CWE-416 DeepSeek-R1+NL(S2) pAcc 26.2, Acc 59.8, F1 38.0

Static-analyzer weakness (Table V): CodeGuru CWE-78 Acc 0.48, Prec 0.40, Rec 0.063, F1 0.11, pAcc 0.0; CodeGuru CWE-476 Prec 1.0 but Rec 0.014, pAcc 0.014; most SA tools scored Acc ~0.50 with 0 true positives and pAcc 0 on CWE-190/416
CVEFixes C/C++ vs Khare et al. (Table VI): CWE-78 0.63 to 0.67 (GPT-3.5); CWE-190 0.48 to 0.60 (GPT-4); CWE-416 0.46 to 0.56 (GPT-4); CWE-476 0.58 to 0.59 (GPT-4); up to ~12% accuracy improvement

Manual analysis of 414 LLM responses (Table VII, 46 per combo): e.g. CWE-78 o1+NL(S1) 40 label+reasoning correct / 6 label-correct-reasoning-wrong; CWE-416 GPT-4+NL+CoT(S1) 25 correct vs 21 both-wrong
Scale: over 190,000 API calls across all experiments
baselines_compared: Static analyzers: Amazon CodeGuru Security, Semgrep, CodeQL, SonarQube (default settings, partial-code queries)
Khare et al. prior LLM prompting results on CVEFixes C/C++ (GPT-3.5 / GPT-4)
LLM detectors compared internally: OpenAI o1, Claude 3.5 Sonnet v2, DeepSeek-R1 (671B), plus GPT-4o, GPT-4-turbo, GPT-3.5 on subsets
